tldr: Patched vLLM and GPT-OSS infrastructure to enable concurrent stateful Jupyter sessions and robust code execution for a 120B model, using majority voting over multiple attempts to solve math problems.
summary: The authors leverage a 120B parameter model with integrated code execution via vLLM and the GPT-OSS Harmony protocol. To handle high-concurrency inference efficiently, they patched the default stateless tool server to maintain persistent, per-request Jupyter kernel states. They implemented robustness measures including output truncation, internal execution timeouts via signal.setitimer, and automatic kernel restarts on failure. The final submission uses a simple majority voting strategy across four attempts per question, achieving strong performance on a validation subset.
extensive_summary:
## Infrastructure & Concurrency Patching
The solution leverages a 120B model with code execution via vLLM and GPT-OSS's Harmony protocol. To support high-concurrency inference, the default stateless tool server was patched. `vllm/tools.py` was monkey-patched to inject the `request_id` into the `author.name` field. On the GPT-OSS side, `PythonTool` was modified to read this field, enabling per-request persistent Jupyter kernel state management (creating new sessions or resuming existing ones).

## Robustness & Execution Controls
Several fail-safes were implemented to stabilize code execution: output truncation (first/last 300 characters) to manage context window size, internal timeouts via a `signal.setitimer`-based decorator (`timeoutify`), and automatic kernel restarts upon `KernelDeadError`. A regex post-processor was added to handle formatting inconsistencies like `\displaystyle` vs `\boxed`.

## Inference Strategy & Validation
Inference allocates a flat 10 minutes per question with up to 4 attempts. Final answers are selected via majority voting of non-trivial results, ignoring 0/1 unless they are the only options. Validation on a filtered 226-question subset of IMO-Bench (AnswerBench) on a single B200 GPU showed 83.2% accuracy with 8 attempts using simple majority voting, rising to 84%+ when weighting by tool call counts. Full raw logs were released for community analysis.
ensemble: Majority voting across up to four attempts per question, selecting the most frequent non-trivial result while ignoring 0/1 unless they are the only options.
post_processing: Regex post-processor to capture formatting edge cases like `\displaystyle` instead of `\boxed`.

other_solution_refs.description: Siancy code to reach their highest score
